preamble.summary: Isaiah 33 opens with a woe oracle against the destroyer and pivots into one of the most exalted portraits of the LORD as judge, lawgiver, king, and savior. The chapter moves from crisis to confidence: envoys weep, highways lie waste, the land mourns, yet the LORD rises in majesty. The faithful behold a king in his beauty, see a land that stretches far, and dwell in Zion — the unmovable tent whose stakes will never be pulled up. Sickness itself is forgiven in the final verse.
preamble.remarkable: Verse 22 compresses four titles into a single declaration — the LORD is judge, lawgiver, king, and savior. No human institution can claim all four roles; they belong to God alone. The vision of Zion as an immovable tent (v.20) reverses exile imagery and anchors hope in divine permanence rather than human fortification.
preamble.friction: We have maintained "Woe" for hoi in verse 1 as a covenant-lawsuit interjection rather than softening it. The phrase "your eyes shall behold the king in his beauty" (v.17) is rendered with messianic resonance, as the context transcends any earthly monarch.
preamble.connections:
The destroyer-destroyed pattern of verse 1 echoes the lex talionis principle. The king in his beauty (v.17) anticipates the suffering servant who "had no beauty that we should desire him" (53:2) — the contrast is deliberate within the book. The forgiveness of sickness in verse 24 foreshadows Matthew 9:2-6 where Jesus links forgiveness and healing.

**Tradition comparisons:** The Dead Sea Scrolls (1QIsaiah-a) preserve this chapter with notable variants: Verse 6 has a variant in the treasure/wisdom passage. Verse 14 has the 'sinners in Zion' passage with the everlasting fire question. Verse 22 has the triple designation of the LORD as judge, lawgiver, and king.. See the [DSS Isaiah comparison](/dss-isaiah/33). Targum Jonathan provides interpretive renderings: The triple-office formula (judge, lawgiver/commander, king) is rendered literally. These titles require no anti-anthropomorphic adjustment — they are relational roles, not physical descriptions. See [Targum Jonathan on Isaiah](/targum/isaiah).

verses.text_hebrew: הוֹי שׁוֹדֵד וְאַתָּה לֹא שָׁדוּד וּבוֹגֵד וְלֹא־בָגְדוּ בוֹ כַּהֲתִמְךָ שׁוֹדֵד תּוּשַּׁד כַּנְּלֹתְךָ לִבְגֹד יִבְגְּדוּ־בָךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: Woe to thee that spoilest, and thou wast not spoiled; and dealest treacherously, and they dealt not treacherously with thee! when thou shalt cease to spoil, thou shalt be spoiled; and when thou shalt make an end to deal treacherously, they shall deal treacherously with thee.
verses.rendering:
Woe to you, destroyer,
you who have not been destroyed!
You treacherous one,
whom none has betrayed!
When you finish destroying,
you shall be destroyed;
when you cease your treachery,
they shall deal treacherously with you.

key_terms.note: Hoi introduces a prophetic judgment oracle — both a cry of grief and a legal indictment.

translator_notes: The woe oracle targets a destroyer who has escaped destruction — almost certainly Assyria. The divine justice principle is clear: the measure you give will be the measure you receive.

verses.text_hebrew: יְהוָה חָנֵּנוּ לְךָ קִוִּינוּ הֱיֵה זְרֹעָם לַבְּקָרִים אַף־יְשׁוּעָתֵנוּ בְּעֵת צָרָה׃
verses.text_kjv: O LORD, be gracious unto us; we have waited for thee: be thou their arm every morning, our salvation also in the time of trouble.
verses.rendering:
O LORD, be gracious to us;
we have waited for you.
Be our arm every morning,
our salvation in the time of distress.
translator_notes: The community responds with prayer. "Be our arm" (zeroa) asks God to be their strength — the same divine arm that delivered Israel from Egypt (Exodus 6:6).

verses.text_hebrew: מִקּוֹל הָמוֹן נָדְדוּ עַמִּים מֵרוֹמְמֻתֶךָ נָפְצוּ גּוֹיִם׃
verses.text_kjv: At the noise of the tumult the people fled; at the lifting up of thyself the nations were scattered.
verses.rendering:
At the thunderous noise, peoples flee;
when you rise up, nations scatter.
translator_notes: The subject shifts to the LORD. His rising (romemut) produces the same effect as a roaring army — nations scatter before theophanic power.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאֻסַּף שְׁלַלְכֶם אֹסֶף הֶחָסִיל כְּמַשַּׁק גֵּבִים שׁוֹקֵק בּוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: And your spoil shall be gathered like the gathering of the caterpiller: as the running to and fro of locusts shall he run upon them.
verses.rendering:
Your spoil shall be gathered
as the caterpillar gathers;
as locusts leap upon it,
so shall they leap upon your plunder.
translator_notes: The plunder of the destroyed enemy will be gathered as thoroughly as locusts strip a field — the destroyer's own tactics turned against him.

verses.text_hebrew: נִשְׂגָּב יְהוָה כִּי שֹׁכֵן מָרוֹם מִלֵּא צִיּוֹן מִשְׁפָּט וּצְדָקָה׃
verses.text_kjv: The LORD is exalted; for he dwelleth on high: he hath filled Zion with judgment and righteousness.
verses.rendering:
The LORD is exalted,
for he dwells on high;
he has filled Zion
with justice and righteousness.

key_terms.semantic_range: exalted, set on high, inaccessibly high, secure
key_terms.note: Nisgav conveys both elevation and impregnability — the LORD is not merely high but unassailably so.

translator_notes: The LORD's exaltation is not abstract transcendence but active governance — he fills Zion with justice and righteousness, the very things human rulers have withheld.

verses.text_hebrew: וְהָיָה אֱמוּנַת עִתֶּיךָ חֹסֶן יְשׁוּעֹת חָכְמַת וָדָעַת יִרְאַת יְהוָה הִיא אוֹצָרוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: And wisdom and knowledge shall be the stability of thy times, and strength of salvation: the fear of the LORD is his treasure.
translator_notes: The fear of the LORD (yir'at YHWH) is called his "treasure" (otsar). In a chapter about plundered wealth, the true treasure is covenantal reverence.

verses.text_hebrew: הֵן אֶרְאֶלָּם צָעֲקוּ חֻצָה מַלְאֲכֵי שָׁלוֹם מַר יִבְכָּיוּן׃
verses.text_kjv: Behold, their valiant ones shall cry without: the ambassadors of peace shall weep bitterly.
verses.rendering:
Behold, their valiant ones cry out in the streets;
the envoys of peace weep bitterly.
translator_notes: The er'elam ("valiant ones") are likely Jerusalem's warriors or diplomats. Even the bravest weep — the diplomatic situation is hopeless by human measure.

verses.text_hebrew: נָשַׁמּוּ מְסִלּוֹת שָׁבַת עֹבֵר אֹרַח הֵפֵר בְּרִית מָאַס עָרִים לֹא חָשַׁב אֱנוֹשׁ׃
verses.text_kjv: The highways lie waste, the wayfaring man ceaseth: he hath broken the covenant, he hath despised the cities, he regardeth no man.
verses.rendering:
The highways lie desolate,
the traveler has ceased.
He has broken the covenant,
he has despised the cities,
he regards no one.
translator_notes: Desolate highways signal collapsed commerce and communication. The covenant-breaker treats treaties, cities, and human life with equal contempt.

verses.text_hebrew: אָבַל אֻמְלְלָה אָרֶץ הֶחְפִּיר לְבָנוֹן קָמַל הָיָה הַשָּׁרוֹן כָּעֲרָבָה וְנֹעֵר בָּשָׁן וְכַרְמֶל׃
verses.text_kjv: The earth mourneth and languisheth: Lebanon is ashamed and hewn down: Sharon is like a wilderness; and Bashan and Carmel shake off their fruits.
verses.rendering:
The land mourns and languishes;
Lebanon is ashamed and withers.
Sharon has become like a desert,
and Bashan and Carmel shake off their leaves.
translator_notes: Four of Israel's most fertile regions — Lebanon, Sharon, Bashan, Carmel — are devastated. The land itself participates in the mourning (see Hosea 4:3).

verses.text_hebrew: עַתָּה אָקוּם יֹאמַר יְהוָה עַתָּה אֵרוֹמָם עַתָּה אֶנָּשֵׂא׃
verses.text_kjv: Now will I rise, saith the LORD; now will I be exalted; now will I lift up myself.
translator_notes: The triple "now" (attah) marks dramatic divine intervention. After patient waiting through human crisis, the LORD announces his own rising.

verses.text_hebrew: תַּהֲרוּ חֲשַׁשׁ תֵּלְדוּ קַשׁ רוּחֲכֶם אֵשׁ תֹּאכַלְכֶם׃
verses.text_kjv: Ye shall conceive chaff, ye shall bring forth stubble: your breath, as fire, shall devour you.
verses.rendering:
You conceive chaff, you bring forth stubble;
your own breath is a fire that shall consume you.
translator_notes: Addressed to the enemies: their schemes produce only worthless material, and their own breath (ruach) becomes the fire of their destruction.

verses.text_hebrew: וְהָיוּ עַמִּים מִשְׂרְפוֹת שִׂיד קוֹצִים כְּסוּחִים בָּאֵשׁ יִצַּתּוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: And the people shall be as the burnings of lime: as thorns cut up shall they be burned in the fire.
verses.rendering:
And the peoples shall be as if burned to lime,
like cut thorns consumed in the fire.
translator_notes: The imagery is of complete combustion — lime-burning requires intense, sustained heat. The thorns echo the curse imagery of 32:13, now applied to God's enemies.

verses.text_hebrew: שִׁמְעוּ רְחוֹקִים אֲשֶׁר עָשִׂיתִי וּדְעוּ קְרוֹבִים גְּבוּרָתִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Hear, ye that are far off, what I have done; and, ye that are near, acknowledge my might.
verses.rendering:
"Hear, you who are far off, what I have done;
and you who are near, acknowledge my might."
translator_notes: The LORD addresses both distant nations and nearby peoples. His acts of judgment are a universal proclamation meant to be known by all.

verses.text_hebrew: פָּחֲדוּ בְצִיּוֹן חַטָּאִים אָחֲזָה רְעָדָה חֲנֵפִים מִי יָגוּר לָנוּ אֵשׁ אוֹכֵלָה מִי יָגוּר לָנוּ מוֹקְדֵי עוֹלָם׃
verses.text_kjv: The sinners in Zion are afraid; fearfulness hath surprised the hypocrites. Who among us shall dwell with the devouring fire? who among us shall dwell with everlasting burnings?
verses.rendering:
The sinners in Zion are terrified;
trembling has seized the godless:
"Who among us can dwell with consuming fire?
Who among us can dwell with everlasting flames?"
translator_notes: The question is about surviving the presence of a holy God. The sinners within the covenant community recognize that God's holiness is itself the consuming fire (see Hebrews 12:29).

verses.text_hebrew: הֹלֵךְ צְדָקוֹת וְדֹבֵר מֵישָׁרִים מֹאֵס בְּבֶצַע מַעֲשַׁקּוֹת נֹעֵר כַּפָּיו מִתְּמֹךְ בַּשֹּׁחַד אֹטֵם אָזְנוֹ מִשְּׁמֹעַ דָּמִים וְעֹצֵם עֵינָיו מֵרְאוֹת בְּרָע׃
verses.text_kjv: He that walketh righteously, and speaketh uprightly; he that despiseth the gain of oppressions, that shaketh his hands from holding of bribes, that stoppeth his ears from hearing of blood, and shutteth his eyes from seeing evil;
verses.rendering:
He who walks in righteousness and speaks what is upright,
who despises the gain of oppression,
who shakes his hands free from holding a bribe,
who stops his ears from hearing of bloodshed,
and shuts his eyes from looking upon evil —
translator_notes: Isaiah's answer to verse 14, structured like an entrance liturgy (compare Psalm 15 and Psalm 24:3-4). Five ethical qualifications describe who can dwell in God's holy presence.

verses.text_hebrew: הוּא מְרוֹמִים יִשְׁכֹּן מְצָדוֹת סְלָעִים מִשְׂגַּבּוֹ לַחְמוֹ נִתָּן מֵימָיו נֶאֱמָנִים׃
verses.text_kjv: He shall dwell on high: his place of defence shall be the munitions of rocks: bread shall be given him; his waters shall be sure.
translator_notes: The righteous receives three provisions: elevated dwelling (safety), bread (sustenance), and reliable water (ongoing life) — covenant blessings where God becomes fortress, provider, and sustainer.

verses.text_hebrew: מֶלֶךְ בְּיָפְיוֹ תֶּחֱזֶינָה עֵינֶיךָ תִּרְאֶינָה אֶרֶץ מַרְחַקִּים׃
verses.text_kjv: Thine eyes shall see the king in his beauty: they shall behold the land that is very far off.
verses.rendering:
Your eyes shall behold the king in his beauty;
they shall see a land that stretches far away.
verses.expanded_rendering: Melekh beyofyo techezenah einekha — "the king in his beauty your eyes shall behold." The faithful one of verses 15-16 receives a vision of the messianic sovereign of 32:1. The erets marchaqim ("land that stretches far away") suggests a kingdom without borders, extending beyond besieged Jerusalem.

key_terms.note: The king's beauty is the visible radiance of righteous rule, standing in deliberate tension with Isaiah 53:2 where the servant has "no beauty that we should desire him."

translator_notes: Those who shut their eyes from evil (v.15) are given eyes to see the king in glory. The movement from moral discipline to beatific vision is profoundly theological.

verses.text_hebrew: לִבְּךָ יֶהְגֶּה אֵימָה אַיֵּה סֹפֵר אַיֵּה שֹׁקֵל אַיֵּה סֹפֵר אֶת־הַמִּגְדָּלִים׃
verses.text_kjv: Thine heart shall meditate terror. Where is the scribe? where is the receiver? where is he that counted the towers?
verses.rendering:
Your heart shall muse on the former terror:
"Where is the one who counted?
Where is the one who weighed?
Where is the one who counted the towers?"
translator_notes: The rhetorical questions recall Assyrian officials who tallied tribute, weighed silver, and surveyed fortifications. In the age of the king's beauty, these oppressors are simply gone.

verses.text_hebrew: אֶת־עַם נוֹעָז לֹא תִרְאֶה עַם עִמְקֵי שָׂפָה מִשְּׁמוֹעַ נִלְעַג לָשׁוֹן אֵין בִּינָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou shalt not see a fierce people, a people of a deeper speech than thou canst perceive; of a stammering tongue, that thou canst not understand.
verses.rendering:
You shall no longer see the fierce people,
the people of obscure speech that you cannot comprehend,
of a stammering tongue that you cannot understand.
translator_notes: The Assyrians spoke Akkadian — foreign and terrifying to Hebrew speakers. In the new Zion, the threatening foreigner with incomprehensible speech is gone.

verses.text_hebrew: חֲזֵה צִיּוֹן קִרְיַת מוֹעֲדֵנוּ עֵינֶיךָ תִרְאֶינָה יְרוּשָׁלַ͏ִם נָוֶה שַׁאֲנָן אֹהֶל בַּל־יִצְעָן בַּל־יִסַּע יְתֵדֹתָיו לָנֶצַח וְכָל־חֲבָלָיו בַּל־יִנָּתֵקוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Look upon Zion, the city of our solemnities: thine eyes shall see Jerusalem a quiet habitation, a tabernacle that shall not be taken down; not one of the stakes thereof shall ever be removed, neither shall any of the cords thereof be broken.
verses.rendering:
Gaze upon Zion, the city of our appointed feasts!
Your eyes shall see Jerusalem —
a quiet habitation,
a tent that shall not be moved:
its stakes shall never be pulled up,
nor any of its cords be broken.
verses.expanded_rendering: The image of Zion as ohel bal-yits'an ("a tent that shall not be moved") is stunning. Tents are by nature temporary, yet this tent is permanent — its yetedotav (stakes) will never be pulled up, its chavalav (cords) never snapped. The tabernacle theology is unmistakable: God's dwelling with his people is now anchored forever in Zion.
translator_notes: The command to "gaze" (chazeh) uses the same root as the prophetic word for vision (chazon). Jerusalem is both the city of appointed feasts (mo'adenu) and an immovable tent — worship calendar and divine presence converge.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּי אִם־שָׁם אַדִּיר יְהוָה לָנוּ מְקוֹם־נְהָרִים יְאֹרִים רַחֲבֵי יָדָיִם בַּל־תֵּלֶךְ בּוֹ אֳנִי־שַׁיִט וְצִי אַדִּיר לֹא יַעַבְרֶנּוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: But there the glorious LORD will be unto us a place of broad rivers and streams; wherein shall go no galley with oars, neither shall gallant ship pass thereby.
verses.rendering:
But there the LORD in majesty shall be for us
a place of broad rivers and streams,
where no galley with oars shall go,
and no mighty ship shall pass.
translator_notes: Jerusalem lacked a great river unlike Babylon, Egypt, or Nineveh. The LORD himself becomes Zion's river — providing what geography denied, yet admitting no warships.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּי יְהוָה שֹׁפְטֵנוּ יְהוָה מְחֹקְקֵנוּ יְהוָה מַלְכֵּנוּ הוּא יוֹשִׁיעֵנוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: For the LORD is our judge, the LORD is our lawgiver, the LORD is our king; he will save us.
verses.rendering:
For the LORD is our judge,
the LORD is our lawgiver,
the LORD is our king —
he will save us.
verses.expanded_rendering: YHWH shoftenu, YHWH mechoqeqenu, YHWH malkenu, hu yoshi'enu — four titles in a single verse. The LORD is shofet (judge), mechoqeq (lawgiver), melekh (king), and moshia (savior). No human government combines all four without corruption; they belong to God alone.

key_terms.semantic_range: lawgiver, statute-maker, one who inscribes, ruler
key_terms.note: From choq (statute). The LORD is the source and inscriber of law itself — legislative, judicial, executive, and salvific authority all reside in one person.

translator_notes: One of the most theologically compressed verses in Isaiah. It anticipates the understanding of Christ as prophet, priest, and king while adding the legislative function.

verses.text_hebrew: נִטְּשׁוּ חֲבָלָיִךְ בַּל־יְחַזְּקוּ כֵן־תָּרְנָם בַּל־פָּרְשׂוּ נֵס אָז חֻלַּק עַד־שָׁלָל מַרְבָּה פִּסְחִים בָּזְזוּ בַז׃
verses.text_kjv: Thy tacklings are loosed; they could not well strengthen their mast, they could not spread the sail: then is the prey of a great spoil divided; the lame take the prey.
verses.rendering:
Your rigging hangs loose;
it cannot hold the mast firm
or spread the sail.
Then an abundance of spoil shall be divided —
even the lame shall carry off plunder.
translator_notes: The ship metaphor from verse 21 is applied to the enemy: their vessel is crippled. The spoil is so vast that even the lame (pisechim) take their share, echoing 1 Samuel 30:24.

verses.text_hebrew: וּבַל־יֹאמַר שָׁכֵן חָלִיתִי הָעָם הַיֹּשֵׁב בָּהּ נְשֻׂא עָוֺן׃
verses.text_kjv: And the inhabitant shall not say, I am sick: the people that dwell therein shall be forgiven their iniquity.
verses.rendering:
And no inhabitant shall say, "I am sick";
the people who dwell there shall be forgiven their iniquity.
translator_notes: The chapter's final verse links physical healing to spiritual forgiveness — a connection Jesus explicitly claims in Mark 2:5-12. The word nesu ("forgiven," literally "lifted, carried away") uses the same root as the scapegoat bearing sin in Leviticus 16:22.
